title: Anger at God for the Diagnosis
dek: When the diagnosis drops, the body remembers the betrayal. This is not a test of faith; it is a biological shock that demands your righteous fury. The sudden tightening of the diaphragm, the clenching of the jaw, the cold sweat behind your shoulder blades—these are not signs of weakness. They are the body's primal revolt against an unseen power. In these moments, the body knows truths words cannot capture. Your fury is not ugly. It is alive. It is real. It will burn hot and fierce in the hours after the diagnosis, refusing to be silenced by platitudes or forced optimism. This is your body's way of saying, 'I am here. I am not done.' It is the first pulse of resistance, the raw edge of survival. In this moment, honor it. Let it be. The universe may feel like a cruel place, but the fire blazing inside you is your own fierce affirmation that you are still breathing, still fighting.
bodyMarkdown:
You’re sitting in a sterile room, the fluorescent lights humming overhead like a distant storm. The doctor’s words land in your lap like a stone—diagnosis. Maybe it’s a chronic illness, maybe something you never saw coming. The moment the news hits, it’s as if a cold sweat breaks out behind your shoulder blades, where the trapezius muscle tightens like steel cables pulled taut. Your breath catches, the diaphragm spasming unpredictably, constricted by an invisible hand. The air feels thick, heavy with the metallic scent of antiseptic and your own rapid heartbeat. Somewhere in the back of your throat, the masseter muscle clenches fiercely, the jaw locking in a silent scream you cannot release.

You know this feeling—the sudden betrayal. Not just of your body, but of the divine, the universe, the very fabric of your understanding. You had trusted that there was some benevolent force at play, or at least a fair cosmos, and now it feels like that trust has been ripped away in one jagged tear. The ringing in your ears becomes a harsh soundtrack to the storm inside you, a high-pitched drone that drowns out calm and invites fury. This is not just anger; it’s a primal revolt. You lash out with your mind, throwing blame at God, at fate, at whatever unseen power you once leaned on in the dark hours.

In these moments, the body knows truths words cannot capture. The levator scapulae muscles—those thin ropes running from your neck to your shoulders—bunch up, sending sharp reminders that this is not a gentle grief. It is raw, unfiltered rage. The diaphragm’s contraction restricts your breath, tightening the cage around your heart. Your skin prickles with goosebumps, as if your nervous system itself is screaming in protest. The chest feels constricted, as if the very air around you has thickened into a suffocating fog.

Teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh remind us that the body carries all these stories—the jaw remembers what the mind tries to forget. This is no pathology; this is biology. Robert Augustus Masters speaks to the necessity of holding this anger, not shying away from it, but listening deeply to its message. Your fury is not ugly. It is alive. It is real. It will burn hot and fierce in the hours after the diagnosis, refusing to be silenced by platitudes or forced optimism.

You might find yourself pacing the room in the late-night hours, the cold air of the windowpane brushing against your skin, grounding you just enough to stay present. Your palms sweat, your muscles twitch involuntarily, a physical testament to the internal upheaval. This anger is your body’s way of saying, “I am here. I am not done.” It is the first pulse of resistance, the raw edge of survival. In this moment, honor it. Let it be. The universe may feel like a cruel place, but the fire blazing inside you is your own fierce affirmation that you are still breathing, still fighting.

## The Anatomy of Divine Betrayal: How Anger at God Lives in the Body

It’s late in the evening, just past the hour when the sky is a bruised purple and the air holds the sharpness of impending frost. You sit on the edge of your bed, the weight of your diagnosis pressing down like a stone in your gut. The psoas muscles—those deep, hidden fibers running alongside your lumbar spine—contract fiercely, gripping your lower back in a silent, unyielding protest. This is the body’s way of holding your fury, a muscle that remembers betrayal as if it were a physical wound. Your breath is shallow, caught high in the chest where the diaphragm refuses to expand fully, as if the very act of breathing deeply might release the rage you desperately try to suppress.

Gabor Maté’s work often reminds us that this is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows what the mind cannot fully comprehend, and emotional suppression—especially the kind born from feeling punished by the divine—finds refuge in muscle and fascia. When you’ve been taught that anger, especially at God, is ugly or sinful, your body becomes a vault for that forbidden fire. The psoas, often called the “muscle of the soul,” tightens because it is guarding your vulnerability. It is your body’s way of saying, “I feel betrayed. I feel abandoned.” But it can’t say these words aloud, so it clenches, contracts, and constricts.

The shallow breathing isn’t just a quirk of stress; it is the diaphragm’s subtle rebellion. This dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs is supposed to expand and contract rhythmically, bringing life-sustaining oxygen deep into your system. But when the mind is overwhelmed by the injustice of a diagnosis, the diaphragm tightens, trapping the breath in the upper chest and leaving you gasping for relief that doesn’t come. This is the body’s echo of your inner cry: “Why me? How could this happen to me? To my faith?”

You might notice the tension creeping up your trapezius and levator scapulae, those muscles along the neck and shoulders, pulling your head down as if to shield you from the world’s eyes. Your jaw—the masseter muscle—grits so tightly that it aches, a physical manifestation of resisting the urge to scream or sob. This is the anatomy of divine betrayal: every muscle, every breath, every aching nerve a testament to the rawness of your rage.

Maté’s perspective helps you see this not as weakness or failure but as the body’s desperate attempt to survive emotional pain. The stress of feeling punished by God, of wrestling with a diagnosis that feels like a cosmic injustice, seeps into your tissues. It becomes inflammation, fatigue, and sometimes illness, because the body does not distinguish between emotional and physical harm. The same systems that respond to a physical threat respond to the betrayal you feel in your soul.

This is why tending to that anger—naming it, feeling it fully in the body rather than pushing it away—is a vital act of self-healing. The muscles that tighten in rage need to be met with compassionate awareness, not judgment. Your body remembers the betrayal long after your mind has tried to move on. The path forward is not in silencing that fury but in listening deeply to what your body is telling you. The psoas tightness, the shallow breath, the clenched jaw—these are your body’s prayers, raw and unfiltered, demanding to be seen and held.

## The Righteous Fury: Your Body’s Demand to Be Angry at God Right Now

You are not crazy for feeling furious. That tight coil in your solar plexus—the way your diaphragm clamps down as if it’s holding a scream hostage—that’s your body’s urgent signal. This is not pathology. This is biology. When you receive a diagnosis that feels like a death sentence or a betrayal, your body knows exactly what to do: it demands to scream at the heavens, to lash out at God, at fate, at the universe that allowed this to happen. You can almost taste the metallic tang of adrenaline pooling on your tongue, the way the masseter muscles in your jaw clench so tightly they throb through your temples. This is not something to suppress or apologize for; it is a necessary, boundary-restoring fire.

Karla McLaren, whose work on anger I hold near, describes anger as a boundary-restoring emotion. It is the body’s way of saying, “No more. This is not okay.” When your world tilts on its axis with a diagnosis that feels unjust, your anger is the perimeter guard, the sentry that refuses to let despair or numbness rush in unchecked. It’s the body’s way of marking its territory, saying, “You crossed a line.” Your trapezius muscles, those broad sheets of muscle from neck to shoulder, tighten involuntarily, bracing you against the injustice, while your levator scapulae hitch up as if trying to shrug off the weight of this unbearable news.

The need to scream is not metaphorical. It’s as real as the night air brushing cold against your skin at 3 a.m., when the house is silent except for the occasional creak of settling wood. Your diaphragm spasms, the muscle thick and powerful beneath your ribs, contracting fiercely as if it could force the sound out through the tightly sealed jaw. The body remembers, even when your mind wants to look away. Remember how Robert Augustus Masters talks about the jaw as a repository of unexpressed fury? That clenched masseter is a fortress holding back the storm. Let it tremble. Let it shake. Let the body have its moment of rage.

This rage is sacred. It is the body’s ancient way of rallying strength, not just to survive, but to fight back. Pema Chödrön’s teachings on touching your edge don’t mean softening into passivity but leaning into the fierce edges of your own experience. You are allowed to be angry at God because your body needs that anger to reset its boundaries, to reclaim its space in a world that suddenly feels dangerously out of control.

This is not a failure of faith or character. This is your biology screaming, “I am here. I am alive. I demand to be seen.” Your body needs this storm to clear the toxic fog of helplessness and despair. It needs to flex, to roar, to let every muscle fiber—from the tightening psoas deep in your abdomen to the trembling fingers—know that it will not be silenced. In this sacred fury, you find the first pulse of your own fierce, raw power.

## Brewing Calm in the Midnight Rage

You find yourself awake at 3 AM, the clock’s red glow bleeding into the dark room, your jaw clenched so tightly the masseter muscle hums with tension. The air is cool, damp with the faint scent of rain seeping through the cracked window frame. Your body thrums with a raw, thudding anger—not just at the diagnosis itself but at the cosmos, at the silent God you once trusted. This is not pathology; this is biology reacting to shock. The diaphragm tightens, trapping your breath, and the levator scapulae knot up in your neck, a fortress of tension built from fury. To soothe this storm, you reach for the herbs: motherwort, skullcap, and milky oats—each a balm for the nervous system’s raw edges.

Motherwort, Leonurus cardiaca, smells faintly of green leaves pressed between pages, a subtle bitterness that hints at the iron-rich earth it comes from. It carries a whisper of ancient wisdom, a lineage of soothing the heart and calming the pulse when it races with grief or rage. As you steep the crushed leaves in hot water, the aroma unfolds—herbal, slightly astringent, like a forest floor after rain. The first sip is sharp, almost wild on your tongue, a little like bitter greens, but then it settles, a coolness spreading down the throat, riding the tight bands of fascia that wrap around your chest. You feel your pulse slow, the diaphragm gently loosening from its constriction, even as your anger simmers in the background, less a wildfire now and more a controlled burn.

Skullcap, Scutellaria lateriflora, brings a different kind of relief. Its scent is soft and grassy, a hint of lavender’s cousin, but more subdued, more grounded. When you drink the infusion, the liquid is smooth, almost sweet, sliding over the muscles of your throat and chest like a silken ribbon. The trapezius, so often clenched in moments like these, begins to release its iron grip. The nervous system, frayed and screaming, finds a moment’s pause. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches us to breathe into the body’s pain, and with skullcap, you can almost hear that invitation whispered in every molecule, coaxing your system to settle, to come home.

Milky oats, Avena sativa, taste like a warm, gentle earthiness, a comfort from the fields and sun. The infusion is golden, glowing softly in the dim light, and as you swallow, the sensation is like a soft hand on the back of your neck, easing the tension in the psoas—the muscle often holding the story of trauma and shock in its deep coils. The oats bring nourishment to the nervous system, a medicine for the sleepless hours when the mind churns with “why” and “how could this be.” Drinking milky oats at this hour is a radical act of kindness to yourself, an acknowledgment that the body remembers what the heart has yet to accept.

Together, these herbs form a triad of support, a grounding ritual in the isolation of your midnight fury. The taste, the smell, the feeling of the warm liquid threading through your muscles and fascia is an unspoken conversation between you and your body. This is where the jaw remembers, where the diaphragm sighs, where the anger, though not gone, is held with fierce tenderness. You are not alone in this dark hour; you have this medicine—the living, breathing kindness of plants—and the knowledge that even in shock, the body knows how to heal.

## Rooting Fury into the Earth: An Embodied Practice for Releasing Anger at God

You arrive at this moment with a body full of tension, a clenched jaw, and a heart that feels like it’s been slammed into a wall. The fury at God for the diagnosis—whether whispered in your mind or screamed in the dark—presses down heavy in your chest, tangled in the diaphragm, tightening the trapezius and levator scapulae like a vice. This is not a failure of faith or a moral flaw; this is biology speaking in the language of your body, and it deserves to be heard, moved, and released.

Begin by standing barefoot on the floor, ideally on cool earth, grass, or a wooden floor that still holds the faint scent of pine or cedar. The moment could be early morning, when the air is crisp and the world is still holding its breath, or late at night when silence magnifies every internal sensation. Feel the soles of your feet connect to the ground. Sense the weight pressing down into the metatarsal heads, the ball of your foot, and the heel—these three points forming a tripod that roots you firmly into the earth. The fascia beneath your feet, the plantar fascia, begins to spread and soften, like roots burrowing deeper into soil. Allow the subtle shift of weight from side to side, front to back, to awaken the muscles of your calves and shins, gently coaxing the tibialis anterior and the gastrocnemius to release held tightness.

Now, drop your hands to your sides or place them on your hips—whatever feels less guarded. Tilt your chin slightly up, opening the front of your throat, loosening the masseter muscles that have clenched so tightly around your fury. Take a slow, deliberate breath, noticing the diaphragm expanding as it pushes down into the abdominal cavity, the ribs widening, the belly softening. Let this breath be a silent invitation to your body to begin shifting.

Without any words, begin to vocalize the rage as a raw sound—an “ah,” a guttural “grrr,” or even a low, rumbling growl. There is no need for language here. This is not about forming sentences or explaining your pain. The sound should start low in your belly, felt deep in the psoas muscle, and release upward through the throat and into the open mouth. Feel your neck muscles soften as you make space for the sound to flow. Your voice becomes a river of rage, moving through the body, shaking loose the jaw, the tongue, the tight fascia in the throat. If it wants to be louder, let it. If it wants to be a whisper, honor that too.

As you vocalize, sense the subtle vibrations traveling down your arms and into your fingers, the tremors traveling up your spine, the loosening of the trapezius that’s been bracing against the betrayal you feel. This is the body remembering how to express what words cannot hold. When you feel the sound begin to fade, close your mouth, and return to the breath. Notice any shifts in tension or space inside your chest, your neck, your belly.

You are not alone in this practice. Teachers like Karla McLaren remind us that anger is a sacred energy, meant to protect and empower, not to be shamed or suppressed. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that by grounding in the body and the earth, we find a sanctuary within ourselves that can hold even the most devastating of truths. This practice is your sanctuary—a place where fury can be both witnessed and released, where the jaw remembers that it does not need to stay clenched, and the feet know they are not standing on quicksand but on solid ground.

Take this practice with you, return to it in the solitude of early morning or the quiet of late night, and let the body do what it must to be free. This is not pathology; this is biology finding its voice again.

## FAQ

**Q: I’m so angry at God for this diagnosis. I feel guilty for feeling that way. Is it wrong to be angry at the divine?**

A: First, let’s take a breath together. Breathe all the way down to your diaphragm, feel it drop, the soft expansion beneath your ribs. That anger you’re holding isn’t a moral failing. It’s a living, pulsing response lodged deep in your body—maybe in the tightness of your masseter muscle, clenched jaw refusing to let go, or the knot in your psoas that’s been gripping since you heard the news. Your anger is not a sin or a betrayal. It’s biology. It’s the body remembering the injustice, the rupture of your world. Teachers like Karla McLaren remind us that anger is a vital emotion, a protector, a healer when understood. You’re allowed this fury. You are allowed to direct it at God if that is where your heart lands because the divine, in many traditions, is vast enough to hold your rage and your grief simultaneously. The jaw remembers far longer than your thoughts do; listen to what it’s telling you rather than shaming yourself for what you feel.

**Q: I’m scared that if I let myself be angry, I’ll make the illness worse. Negative emotions must be bad for my body, right?**

A: This is a fear that echoes in many of us, especially women taught to smooth over their edges and keep the peace inside. But the body does not confuse anger with pathology—it’s the chronic suppression and dissociation from your emotions that lead to trouble. Think of the levator scapulae muscle, how it tightens and twists when you hold back fury; that tension is where trauma and illness like to nest. Gabor Maté and Bessel van der Kolk teach us that unresolved emotions are often the soil in which physical symptoms grow. But anger itself, unexpressed and unacknowledged, is the real danger. When you allow yourself to feel anger, to breathe into it, to move it through your body—even if it feels like a storm swirling in your belly—you’re engaging your nervous system in a dance towards regulation. It’s not about fostering negativity; it’s about honest presence. Pema Chödrön’s guidance on sitting with uncomfortable emotions without judgment is key here. It’s not the anger that harms you, it’s the silence around it.

**Q: How can I hold both the illness and the anger without feeling overwhelmed or like I’m betraying my healing?**

A: This is where the body’s wisdom becomes your ally. You can feel the illness in your limbs, in the dull ache behind your eyes, in the fatigue wrapping your trapezius like a heavy cloak—and still make space for anger. These are not mutually exclusive experiences. Try this: when you notice the illness pressing in, place a hand gently over your solar plexus and acknowledge that presence. Then, shift your awareness to the heat gathering in your chest or the tension in your masseter, naming the anger without trying to fix or banish it. Resmaa Menakem’s work on embodied racial trauma shows us that holding conflicting sensations, sitting with them in the body, builds resilience. This practice doesn’t mean you surrender to the overwhelm; it means you become fluent in the language of your body’s complexity. You are not betraying your healing by being angry. You are honoring your whole self—the part that hurts, the part that fights, the part that longs for justice. The body knows how to integrate this. The jaw remembers. Let it speak.
